segments.title: What should the interwar Royal Navy carrier have been?
segments.summary: Drachinifel answers Dr Clarke's opening design question by proposing Admiral-class battlecruiser hulls as the Royal Navy's large interwar carriers.
segments.body: The answer begins with a plausible divergence rather than a clean-sheet fantasy: retain two Admiral-class hulls long enough to convert them under the Washington treaty rules. Larger, faster carriers comparable in broad role to Lexington, Saratoga, Akagi, and Kaga would give the Royal Navy a much higher baseline for aircraft capacity, protection, and fleet speed. The trade would probably cost Courageous and Glorious as operational warships, while Furious remained available under the experimental-carrier provisions.
segments.question: What would the ideal pre-Second World War aircraft carrier have looked like for the Royal Navy?
segments.answerShort: Convert two surviving Admiral-class battlecruiser hulls into large carriers, accepting the loss of Courageous and Glorious to gain a faster, more capable interwar carrier core.

evidence.note: Dr Clarke asks for the ideal pre-war carrier; Drachinifel proposes retaining and converting Admiral-class hulls.

segments.title: How large carriers could have strengthened the Fleet Air Arm
segments.summary: Large Admiral-class conversions might have raised naval aviation's institutional status and improved the Royal Navy's influence over aircraft requirements.
segments.body: Ships carrying prestigious names such as Howe and Rodney would likely have justified an earlier rear admiral responsible for aircraft carriers. The historians argue that a larger naval air establishment would also have made the case for separate Fleet Air Arm aircraft designs and direct engineering links with industry. Under the historical arrangement, requirements passed through several naval, Air Ministry, and industrial layers, creating repeated opportunities for distortion even when everyone involved acted in good faith.

evidence.note: The discussion connects carrier size and naming to senior command status, Fleet Air Arm scale, engineering expertise, and procurement communication.

segments.title: A layered interwar carrier fleet
segments.summary: The alternate fleet assigns older and smaller carriers to support, training, cruiser, and regional duties around two large strike carriers.
segments.body: Capability differences would encourage clearer specialization. Furious and Eagle could replenish or support the big carriers, Hermes could continue cruiser-carrier duties on distant stations, and Argus might be sacrificed earlier to treaty tonnage. The discussion also notes that Argus historically operated Queen Bee target drones, complicating modern claims about the first drone carrier.

evidence.note: The historians allocate distinct support and cruiser-carrier functions and identify Argus's Queen Bee drone role.

segments.title: Large hangars and twin-engine carrier aircraft
segments.summary: Oversized early carriers might have encouraged twin-engine naval aircraft, taller hangars, and experiments with compact deck-edge lifts.
segments.body: A carrier able to house more aircraft than its crew could efficiently operate might redirect spare volume toward fewer, larger twin-engine strike aircraft or fighters. That choice would influence Ark Royal's later hangar proportions and perhaps encourage an external lift isolated from the enclosed hangar by substantial doors. The Blackburn Blackburn appears as an ungainly but important example of airborne spotting and warning functions that later became central to carrier operations.

evidence.note: The speakers connect large carrier volume to twin-engine aircraft, hangar choices, deck-edge lifts, and the Blackburn Blackburn's spotting role.

segments.title: Strike, fleet, and cruiser carrier doctrine
segments.summary: Dr Clarke explains the Royal Navy's distinct strike-carrier, fleet-carrier, and cruiser-carrier concepts between the wars.
segments.body: Cruiser carriers supported trade protection and cruiser operations; fleet carriers supplied reconnaissance, fighters, spotting, and some attack power; strike carriers delivered the major offensive blow. Ark Royal therefore reflected a specifically British strike-carrier lineage rather than simply copying American or Japanese practice. British doctrine also emphasized a continuing flow of smaller aircraft groups, although it retained the ability to assemble a larger strike.

evidence.note: Dr Clarke defines the three carrier roles and places Ark Royal in the strike-carrier tradition.

segments.title: Armored Ark Royals and multi-carrier operations
segments.summary: The ideal late-interwar design combines Ark Royal's air group with armor, while larger surviving carriers make multi-carrier operations more attainable.
segments.body: The historians describe something close to an Implacable-sized armored Ark Royal: enough aircraft for both fleet and strike roles, with protection suited to distant theaters where major repairs were unavailable. Experience with very large carriers might also have made the Royal Navy more willing to accept fewer hulls with bigger air groups. Durable Admiral-class conversions could survive damage that doomed Courageous and might have avoided the circumstances that exposed Glorious, while smaller Hermes-like ships handled convoy and anti-submarine duties.

evidence.note: The discussion covers armored Ark Royal concepts, carrier numbers, multi-carrier doctrine, survivability, and smaller ASW carriers.

segments.title: Why Malta and Midway converged
segments.summary: British Malta and American Midway designs approached a common compromise between armored flight decks, large air groups, and ventilated hangars.
segments.body: Wartime operations exposed weaknesses in both national design traditions. British enclosed armored-box hangars restricted capacity and engine operation, while American carriers risked catastrophic losses when bombs or kamikazes reached crowded aircraft and fuel on the flight deck. Malta and Midway consequently moved toward armored flight decks combined with hangars that allowed airflow, preserving protection without fully enclosing the aircraft spaces.

evidence.note: The historians compare British and American wartime experience and the resulting Malta/Midway design convergence.

segments.title: False lessons from carrier exercises
segments.summary: Artificial exercise boundaries could exaggerate the ability of cruisers to find carriers and distort which ships attacking aircraft would choose.
segments.body: Exercise pilots often sought prestigious capital ships instead of attacking the first worthwhile target, while cruisers benefited from knowing the limited operating area in which a carrier had to be found. Real combat removed both assumptions: pilots facing lethal defenses might attack an exposed destroyer, and carriers could maneuver across a much larger sea area. The lesson is not to dismiss exercises, but to separate genuine operational insight from behavior created by safety rules and scenario boundaries.

evidence.note: Dr Clarke explains how target incentives and defined exercise areas can generate misleading results.

segments.title: Which carriers should postwar Britain retain?
segments.summary: The historians inventory Britain's crowded postwar carrier fleet and distinguish worn wartime ships from hulls worth developing for the jet age.
segments.body: Drachinifel asks what the surviving fleet should look like before considering new construction. The Illustrious class had cramped armored hangars and heavily worn machinery, making training, reserve, museum, and disposal roles more sensible than costly reconstruction. The newer Implacables could bridge the immediate gap, while the numerous light carriers, Audacious hulls, and Malta designs supplied experiments and future capacity.
segments.question: What should the Royal Navy's surviving carrier fleet have been immediately after the Second World War?
segments.answerShort: Retire the worn Illustrious class from front-line service, retain the newer Implacables temporarily, and use the light carriers, Audacious hulls, and Malta designs to build the jet-age force.

evidence.note: Drachinifel asks for the postwar fleet baseline and the answer assesses each surviving or building class.

segments.title: Use light carriers to perfect the jet-age fleet
segments.summary: A staged program uses light carriers for experiments, completes Audacious and Malta hulls with mature jet-age features, and assigns Centaurs to amphibious roles.
segments.body: The proposal deliberately slows Malta construction while accelerating two Audacious ships as conventional straight-deck carriers. Colossus and Majestic hulls become inexpensive test beds for steam catapults, angled decks, and other emerging systems. Once the configuration is proven, two more Audacious ships and four Maltas enter service with angled decks from the outset, while several Centaurs serve as helicopter, amphibious-assault, and command ships.

evidence.note: The speakers lay out a staged construction and experimentation program for Audacious, Malta, light-carrier, and Centaur hulls.

segments.title: Designing a carrier fleet to survive Treasury cuts
segments.summary: A layered fleet protects four Malta-class strike carriers by offering less capable ships for successive budget reductions.
segments.body: The force is intentionally built with useful but expendable outer layers. Centaurs can be retired first, straight-deck Audacious ships can take over amphibious duties and later leave service, and angled-deck Audacious ships can move from strike to support and assault roles. Four Malta-class carriers remain the long-lived core, so major reductions in hull numbers do not immediately erase the central strike capability.

evidence.note: The discussion sequences retirements and role changes to protect the Malta-class strike core.

segments.title: An Atlantic carrier navy as a diplomatic instrument
segments.summary: A sustained British carrier force could anchor NATO's Atlantic strike command and become a routine instrument of prime-ministerial signaling.
segments.body: Regular carrier availability would habituate governments to using a carrier group as a visible response to crises, making the force politically harder to cut. Within NATO, Britain could assume a heavier Atlantic role while the United States concentrated more of its fleet in the Pacific and other theaters. The arrangement would serve American interests while giving Britain a distinctive alliance contribution beyond adding another continental army formation.

evidence.note: The historians connect carrier availability with Atlantic command arrangements, alliance burden-sharing, and political signaling.

segments.title: A four-carrier replacement cycle for the 1980s
segments.summary: Four identical new carriers rotate through strike, refit, readiness, and Royal Marines support roles while retaining surge capacity.
segments.body: The proposed replacement for four aging Maltas uses the rule of three while extracting extra utility from the fourth hull. One carrier operates as the principal strike ship, one refits, one remains ready, and the fourth supports the Royal Marines with helicopters, fighters, and airborne early warning. Because all four retain the same catapults and basic configuration, the amphibious-support ship can rapidly return to full fleet-carrier duty in a major crisis.

evidence.note: The speakers outline four identical replacements and their rotating strike, readiness, refit, and amphibious-support assignments.

segments.title: Would a navalized Tornado work?
segments.summary: Dr Clarke asks whether a carrier Tornado was practical and whether it could beat the F-14; Drachinifel rejects the idea as technically possible but strategically pointless.
segments.body: A naval Tornado could be engineered, just as an F-111B physically existed, but carrier landings would demand extensive airframe strengthening and redesign. The discussion stresses that successful cross-service transfers usually move from naval aircraft to land service, as with the Phantom and Buccaneer, because carrier aircraft already embody the harsher structural requirements. Tornado offered too little that a Buccaneer or purpose-built fleet-defense fighter could not provide more sensibly.
segments.question: How practical would a navalized Tornado have been, and would it have been better than the F-14 Tomcat?
segments.answerShort: It was physically possible but would require a major redesign and offered no persuasive advantage over purpose-built naval aircraft such as the Buccaneer, Phantom, or F-14.

evidence.note: Dr Clarke poses the navalized Tornado question and Drachinifel explains the structural and role objections.

segments.title: Phantoms, supersonic Harriers, and Super Jaguars
segments.summary: A larger Fleet Air Arm opens several plausible paths from Phantom and Sea Vixen successors to a naval Super Jaguar or supersonic V/STOL fighter.
segments.body: A sustained carrier program would preserve a stronger British aircraft industry and remove some of the historical pressure that led to improvised solutions. The historians consider a native Sea Vixen successor, conventional Phantoms without the Royal Navy's unusual extended nose gear, the P.1154 supersonic V/STOL concept, and an Anglo-French naval Super Jaguar. Short or vertical takeoff could accelerate fighter launches while catapults remained available for strike aircraft and heavier support types.

evidence.note: The conversation compares several plausible fighter and strike-aircraft paths for a larger postwar Fleet Air Arm.

segments.title: Merchant ships as distributed fighter bases
segments.summary: Supersonic V/STOL fighters could give merchant ships a limited self-defense role against Soviet bomber raids without creating full auxiliary carriers.
segments.body: Large merchant ships would need only a landing pad, fuel, weapons support, and a few fighters rather than a complete catapult carrier conversion. Those aircraft could form the convoy's local reserve while the fleet carrier committed its main air wing against Soviet Backfire bomber regiments. Common aircraft would also allow damaged or displaced fighters to recover to the fleet carrier, creating a distributed but interoperable air-defense system.

evidence.note: The historians sketch merchant-based V/STOL fighters as a local convoy-defense reserve supporting the main carrier group.

segments.title: The catapult-equipped Queen Elizabeth class that might have been
segments.summary: The historians argue for catapults and F-35Cs, then show how hangar width and length create abrupt thresholds in carrier capability.
segments.body: Slow construction made Britain pay roughly the cost of three ships while receiving two, and the speakers favor fitting catapults from the beginning. Carrier dimensions are not smoothly scalable: losing a small amount of hangar width can remove an entire row of aircraft, while a modest increase in length can complete space for another squadron. Folded F-35Cs are narrower than unfolded-wing F-35Bs, but fixed-wing airborne early-warning aircraft would also demand more hangar room.

evidence.note: The discussion favors catapults and explains the stepped relationship between carrier dimensions and aircraft capacity.

segments.title: How dockyards and bases shape carrier design
segments.summary: The Queen Elizabeth design illustrates how construction docks, gates, bridges, channels, and home bases constrain the warship itself.
segments.body: The carrier was shaped around the chosen Rosyth assembly site and its exit route rather than enlarging infrastructure to suit an unconstrained design. Similar limits affected earlier capital ships, including the Admiral and G3 classes. A larger alternate carrier force would also require more basing capacity; the historians suggest Merseyside alongside Portsmouth, with significant consequences for regional employment and political support.

evidence.note: The speakers link Queen Elizabeth dimensions to Rosyth infrastructure and extend the point to historic designs and alternate carrier bases.

segments.title: Using ship names to protect a carrier program
segments.summary: Naming four carriers London, Cardiff, Edinburgh, and Belfast would make cancellations politically costly across the United Kingdom.
segments.body: The proposal adapts an old Royal Navy technique: attach major warships to places whose elected representatives will resist losing them. Capital-city names would distribute the political stakes across England, Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland. The modern Type 26 naming sequence provides a comparable example, especially when construction jobs and a prominent local ship name reinforce each other.

evidence.note: The historians propose capital-city carrier names and compare the political logic with Type 26 frigate naming.

segments.title: Building a mixed crewed and uncrewed carrier air group
segments.summary: Project Ark Royal raises questions about whether uncrewed aircraft should serve as tankers, loyal wingmen, strike platforms, or increasingly autonomous combatants.
segments.body: The speakers caution that a study of possible conversion remains many decisions away from an operational capability. They expect a long mixed-air-group period in which crewed and uncrewed aircraft divide reconnaissance, support, strike, and air-defense tasks. Wartime innovation in Ukraine—especially autonomous threat detection and evasive behavior under jamming—may allow future carrier drones to advance beyond simple weapons carriers or expendable loyal wingmen.

evidence.note: The discussion covers Project Ark Royal, mixed air-group roles, and autonomous drone lessons emerging from Ukraine.

segments.title: Drone-carrying drones and the old bomber problem
segments.summary: A cheap long-range drone could deliver many smaller autonomous drones, recreating the Cold War requirement to destroy bombers before weapon release.
segments.body: Small drones lack the range to threaten carriers far offshore, while larger drones justify engagement by conventional missiles or future directed-energy weapons. A low-cost carrier drone changes that equation by transporting a swarm to the edge of the defended area and releasing it when threatened or on reaching a programmed point. The tactical geometry resembles defense against Soviet Backfire bombers: the defender must attack the delivery platform before it releases numerous harder-to-handle threats.

evidence.note: The historians distinguish drone ranges and develop the drone-carrier analogy to Cold War bomber interception.

segments.title: CV(A)-01 and the cost of abandoning a distinctive NATO role
segments.summary: Their alternate postwar program bypasses CV(A)-01 and highlights how Britain's central-front focus displaced a more distinctive maritime contribution.
segments.body: CV(A)-01 sought much of the capability their proposed Malta-centered program would have established decades earlier, but it arrived after money and political room had narrowed. The defense settlement prioritized Britain's place on NATO's central European front and set the services against one another for funding. In doing so, Britain surrendered much of the maritime capability that could have made it uniquely valuable to an alliance whose largest army still depended on Atlantic reinforcement.

evidence.note: The speakers explain why their postwar plan removes the need for CV(A)-01 and criticize the exclusive central-front emphasis.

segments.title: Carrier hangars, naval diplomacy, and escalation
segments.summary: Carrier hangars support diplomacy as well as aircraft, while a varied fleet gives governments more rungs on the ladder between routine presence and combat.
segments.body: Large hangars can host receptions amid imposing aircraft, turning aviation capacity into a setting for influence and signaling. Before the Second World War, Britain could escalate from a sloop visit through cruisers, squadrons, and battleships in measured steps. A fleet containing both Audacious- and Malta-sized carriers would restore some choice over how much force and political meaning to attach to a deployment rather than treating every carrier dispatch as the same signal.

evidence.note: Dr Clarke connects carrier spaces with soft power and compares modern deployments with the older graded system of naval presence.
